coredata.dc:title: Pioneering CPMI framework for accurate state-of-health assessment in Lithium ion battery power management using FBG sensors

Ahmad, Munir
coredata.dc:description: Continuous monitoring of the State of Health (SOH) in Lithium-ion (Li-ion) batteries is crucial for ensuring operational reliability and safety in powered devices. This paper presents a novel Classifier-Pursued Maintenance Index Scheme (CPMI) that leverages Fiber Bragg Grating (FBG) sensor measurements for sustainable SOH monitoring and maintenance scheduling. The CPMI framework processes real-time temperature and strain measurements from strategically placed FBG sensors during charge-discharge cycles to estimate battery capacity degradation and determine maintenance requirements. The proposed system employs a support vector-based classification algorithm that categorizes operational states based on FBG sensor data streams, identifying deviations from optimal temperature and voltage ranges. This classification approach generates a quantitative maintenance index that enables systematic assessment scheduling rather than arbitrary inspections. Experimental validation over 200 charge-discharge cycles demonstrates the CPMI system's effectiveness, achieving a maintenance state identification accuracy of 0.95, 75 % classification success rate, classification latency of 0.1 s, precision exceeding 0.95, and an assessment reliability of 0.98. Integrating FBG sensors with the CPMI framework provides a robust Li-ion battery SOH monitoring solution, enabling predictive maintenance strategies and enhanced power management capabilities. The proposed system demonstrates significant potential for improving battery lifecycle management and operational reliability in various applications.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Continuous monitoring of the State of Health (SOH) in Lithium-ion (Li-ion) batteries is crucial for ensuring operational reliability and safety in powered devices. This paper presents a novel Classifier-Pursued Maintenance Index Scheme (CPMI) that leverages Fiber Bragg Grating (FBG) sensor measurements for sustainable SOH monitoring and maintenance scheduling. The CPMI framework processes real-time temperature and strain measurements from strategically placed FBG sensors during charge-discharge cycles to estimate battery capacity degradation and determine maintenance requirements. The proposed system employs a support vector-based classification algorithm that categorizes operational states based on FBG sensor data streams, identifying deviations from optimal temperature and voltage ranges. This classification approach generates a quantitative maintenance index that enables systematic assessment scheduling rather than arbitrary inspections. Experimental validation over 200 charge-discharge cycles demonstrates the CPMI system's effectiveness, achieving a maintenance state identification accuracy of 0.95, 75 % classification success rate, classification latency of 0.1 s, precision exceeding 0.95, and an assessment reliability of 0.98. Integrating FBG sensors with the CPMI framework provides a robust Li-ion battery SOH monitoring solution, enabling predictive maintenance strategies and enhanced power management capabilities. The proposed system demonstrates significant potential for improving battery lifecycle management and operational reliability in various applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: 1Lithium-ion (Li-ion) batteries are commonly used in healthcare and electronic devices. Li-ion batteries are rechargeable batteries that contain protective circuit boards. State of health (SOH) is a complete physical connection between the cell aging of the batteries [1]. SOH is monitored and analyzed to enhance the feasibility range of Li-ion battery-enabled devices. The SOH detection for Li-ion batteries is an important task that produces optimal information for further processes [2]. The SOH detection provides the exact condition and capability range of the batteries. An improved SOH detection method is used to detect Li-ion batteries' rechargeable quality and capacity [3]. The SOH detection method analyzes the health conditions of the batteries during the charging period. It evaluates the batteries' actual charging and working temperatures [4]. The sensor technology for monitoring lithium battery health and safety, summarizing signal generation mechanism, sensing methods, and future advancements to enhance commercialization and research applications [5].

Maintenance index estimation in Li-ion batteries is a task that estimates the approximate battery percentage level. Li-ion batteries are comparatively low maintenance, which requires a shorter cycling schedule to maintain the percentage level [6]. An optimization model is used for the index estimation process. The aim is to address the health index ratio of the batteries [7]. The optimization model identifies the batteries' degradation trajectory level, producing necessary features for the estimation process [8]. The optimization model predicts the exact SOH and maintenance index ratio of the batteries, enhancing the electronic devices' effectiveness range [9]. A principal correlation analysis (PCA) method is used for index estimation. The PCA method extracts redundant battery features used for the scaling and estimation process [10]. The PCA calculates the maintenance index, which is implemented to improve the speed-up level during the charging process [11]. The incorporation of FBG sensors and attribute information fusion of electric and non-electric variables is analyzed for Li-ion batteries' accurate life cycle temperature monitoring [12]. Machine learning (ML) algorithms and techniques are commonly used for detection and prediction processes. The sensor-based strain monitoring system for prismatic lithium-ion batteries' rupture discs improves safety by detecting abuse early and analyzing the state after misuse [13]. A hybrid ML technique is used for SOH detection in Li-ion batteries. The hybrid technique analyzes the temperature variation, physical condition, and distribution range of the batteries [14]. A feature extraction method is implemented to extract the key parameters and features of the batteries. The hybrid technique uses FBG sensors to evaluate the important details from extracted features [15]. The hybrid technique maximizes the feasibility and accuracy range of SOH detection. The k-nearest neighbor (KNN) algorithm-based predictive method is also used for Li-ion batteries [16]. The KNN algorithm identifies the important characteristics and condition range of the batteries which produce data for further detection process [17]. The enhancement of lithium-ion battery safety by analyzing airflow and state-of-charge effects on smoke and gas diffusion proposes an early detection of safety parameters [18].

The research problem involves accurately estimating the state of health (SOH) of lithium-ion batteries under varying temperature and voltage conditions. Current methods lack precision and efficiency, leading to a maintenance index identification rate of less than 70 %. The challenge is to improve the classification ratio and reduce classification time by at least 10 % while ensuring reliable battery performance over multiple charging and discharging cycles.

Lithium-ion batteries are becoming increasingly critical in many fields, including electric cars, renewable energy storage, and portable devices. Because of this, it is essential to ensure that batteries are safe and last a long time. Battery performance is quite sensitive to temperature, voltage, and charge cycles. Traditional monitoring methods sometimes don't have the accuracy or ability to work in real time, which is needed to find even the slightest signs of degradation. This can cause unexpected failures and shorter battery life. Recent improvements in sensing technologies, especially Fiber Bragg Grating (FBG) sensors, make it possible to monitor important battery parameters like temperature and strain more accurately and without damaging the battery. This gives us more data to use for health evaluation.

This research aims to use the benefits of FBG sensors and smart machine learning algorithms to improve battery health monitoring. The research goal is to give accurate and fast assessments of the battery's state-of-health (SOH) and state-of-charge (SOC) by correctly measuring changes in temperature and voltage and combining these measures with classifiers. This strategy gets around the problems with traditional methods by making predictions more accurate, allowing for proactive maintenance, and eventually increasing battery life. Also, combining fiber optic sensors with AI approaches is a step ahead in making innovative, flexible battery management systems that can meet the needs of modern energy users.

The increasing reliance on Li-ion batteries in various applications necessitates robust health monitoring systems to ensure safety and longevity. Enhance SOH estimation can significantly improve battery management, reduce operational costs, and extend the lifespan of electronic devices. With the use of advanced sensing technologies like FBG aims to develop a more effective solution that addresses existing limitations in battery maintenance practices. This research contributes to the growing demand for sustainable and efficient energy storage solutions.

The key points of this article are given below:
✓
SOH-focused Li-battery performance monitoring, estimation, and aging evaluation
✓
Classifier learning for maintenance index finalization under charging and discharging conditions and related features
✓Dataset inherited analysis and data representations
✓Comparative study using heterogeneous metrics and state-of-art methods

Zhang et al. [19] proposed an enhanced LSTM-based method for State of Health (SOH) estimation in lithium-ion batteries. The method utilizes random segments of charging curves to build a robust ensemble model for accurate SOH estimation. The approach aims to develop a reliable and accurate technique for assessing the SOH of lithium-ion batteries. The proposed method offers a promising solution for effective SOH estimation in lithium-ion batteries.

Peng et al. [20] devised an enhanced Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) neural network for estimating the SOH of Lithium-ion batteries. The primary goal was to improve the accuracy and reliability of SOH estimation in Lithium-ion batteries. The method focuses on describing battery aging by extracting multi-health features such as time, energy, and incremental capacity features. The technique achieved highly accurate and robust Lithium-ion battery SOH.

Xiong et al. [21] proposed AI-driven models that incorporate data that is multidimensional, such as voltage level, current, the outside temperature, and under pressure into defect diagnosis algorithms. Although the fault detection accuracy was enhanced to above 95 % during tests conducted on proprietary a high level of battery datasets, the constraints in real-time scalability and generalisability were brought to light.

Xu et al. [22] proposed a condition monitoring method for lithium-ion batteries using a fractional order multivariate grey model with adaptive variables. The main aim is to better maintain electric vehicle lithium-ion batteries by accurately assessing their health using extracted features. The method proves effective in accurately estimating the state of health of lithium-ion batteries in electric vehicles. The model is made to give accurate and flexible estimates of the health status of lithium-ion batteries.

Chen et al. [23] developed an online Lithium-ion battery SOH estimation method using relevant vector machines and dynamic integration. The goal is to propose an effective approach for online SOH estimation in lithium-ion batteries. The method utilizes a wavelet kernel RVM (Relevance Vector Machine) as a sub-model, optimized with the bat algorithm to improve estimation accuracy. The approach demonstrates enhanced accuracy, generalization ability, and uncertainty expression.

Qi et al. [24] introduced a model for Lithium-ion battery state of health using machine learning and neural networks. The main aim is to evaluate the impact of neural networks on lithium-ion battery health management. The method utilizes various neural network techniques for efficient state-of-health simulation and prediction. The approach showcased high efficiency, low energy consumption, and robustness in Lithium-ion battery state of health modeling.

Ma et al. [25] developed a model to predict the health and remaining life of lithium-ion batteries using neural networks. The method highlights the use of LSTM networks to create a strong and reliable model. The method is tested with data from batteries at different health levels, proving it accurately predicts both SOH and RUL. The method offers potential benefits in terms of cost and complexity reduction in battery management.

Wang et al. [26] suggested a method to calculate lithium-ion battery health using Gaussian process regression and differential thermal voltammetry. The method picks out health features (HFs) and measures how much they're connected to capacity loss using a Pearson correlation coefficient. The GPR algorithm established a robust nonlinear mapping relationship between CHF and SOH. The method demonstrated high accuracy and robustness in estimating the health status of lithium-ion batteries.

Xu et al. [27] presented a method using wavelets to estimate the relative health of lithium-ion battery cells. The approach allows determining SOH differences without calculating individual SOH for each cell, relying solely on battery voltage data. The goal is to assess SOH variances without the need for individual calculations, simplifying the process. The method showcased robustness across various aged battery cells and driving conditions.

Wang et al. [28] designed a method, DRSN-CW-LSTM, for predicting the State of Charge (SOC) and SOH in Li-ion batteries. The technique combines predicted SOH and SOC values, like voltage, current, temperature, and capacity data. The paper aims to propose a joint estimation method for SOC and SOH in Li-ion batteries. The approach achieves high accuracy, measured by Mean Squared Error (MSE) and Mean Absolute Error (MAE).

Fauzi et al. [29] proposed a method using a hierarchical extreme learning machine for online estimation of Lithium-ion battery health. The aim is to enhance the accuracy of state-of-health prediction by leveraging deep learning techniques. The method comprehensively explores data collection, processing, model design, training, and evaluation for accurate battery life prediction. The approach ensures precise and reliable forecasts for Lithium-ion battery health.

Chen et al. [30] created a method to jointly estimate Lithium-ion batteries' state of charge and health using a fractional order model. The approach introduces a new health indicator (HI) based on the increase in mean ohmic resistance to represent battery aging. The indicator is used in offline training to establish the HELM (Hierarchical Extreme Learning Machine) model, capturing the correlation between the health indicator and capacity degradation. The method demonstrates good generalization and reliability across dynamic scenarios.

Xu et al. [31] suggested a method for Li-ion battery state of health prediction using an enhanced extreme learning system. The method employed a fractional order (FO) model to address limitations in existing electrochemical and equivalent circuit models. The technique utilized genetic algorithm optimization for accurate parameter identification in the FO model. As verified through experiments, the method accurately predicted battery health under dynamic operating conditions.

Hou et al. [32] introduced a method based on transfer learning to estimate lithium-ion batteries' state of health. The aim is to accurately predict the real-time SOH of lithium-ion batteries, ensuring the safe operation of associated equipment. The method is designed to indicate the SOH of lithium-ion batteries effectively. It provides an efficient technique for assessing the state of health of Lithium-Ion batteries in real-time.

Ma et al. [33] designed a method for estimating Lithium-ion (Li) battery health using a modified flower pollination technique and temporal convolutional network. The method uses transfer learning and a convolutional neural network (CNN) to extract features from raw charging voltage data automatically. The method provides a reliable way to assess the health of Li batteries with improved accuracy. The method proved more accurate in estimating the state of health of Li batteries.

Zhang et al. [34] proposed a Lithium-ion battery SOH estimate using a modified Flower Pollination Algorithm (MFPA) and temporal convolutional network (TCN). The suggested method combines a TCN with the MFPA to adaptively establish the network structure. The TCN model looks at external sensor data and ohmic resistance paths in the Equivalent Circuit Model (ECM) to accurately estimate the SOH. The proposed method showcases high estimation accuracy. Table 1 summarizes the existing research analysis with its limitations, key area focus, and real-time applicability.

Shen et al. [35] showed a Transformer-based SoC estimation methodology that included an Immersion and Invariance (I&I) adaptive observer. The technique was tested on experimental battery datasets with different load situations, and its estimates showed better accuracy and robustness. However, there is a lack of details about how well the model works in real-world situations that are quite dynamic and noisy.

Li et al. [36] devised a way to estimate SOH using a Bayesian Transformer and Incremental Energy Analysis (IEA). When tested using benchmark datasets, the model showed great accuracy and the ability to measure uncertainty under moderate settings. But it hasn't been thoroughly proven to handle severe temperatures and changing charge and discharge characteristics.

Peng et al. [37] devised a new way to estimate SOH using strain data collected by Fiber Bragg Grating (FBG) sensors. The approach links mechanical strain to battery degradation patterns well, but it is hard to integrate sensors and doesn't work with batteries of different chemistries.

Liu et al. [38] established a fault diagnostic model that uses Dynamic Vision Sensors (DVS) for general machine health monitoring and neuromorphic computing. It has excellent real-time capabilities and defect detection rates on vision datasets. Still, it can't be used directly for lithium-ion battery diagnostics because tuning it for that area is hard.

Existing research analysis from Refs. [19–38] describes several approaches to determining the SOH and RUL of lithium-ion batteries, including state-of-the-art methods such as LSTM networks that integrate machine learning techniques and KNN algorithms. Existing research lacks real-time applicability, and data requirements and computing complexity are standard limits of these investigations. Current research fills these gaps by improving real-time monitoring capabilities with the help of FBG sensors, decreasing data dependency, and optimizing algorithms for efficiency. Its ultimate goal is to offer a better, more universal way to evaluate battery health in various settings.

The dataset named DIB_Data [39] is utilized in this article to validate the proposed scheme's performance. This source provides observed SOH values of Li-battery based on degradation and aging. The data is observed based on voltage dissemination and charging capacity of the batteries. The ambient charging/discharging temperature is 15
° c
to
35 ° c
across 200 cycles. The dissemination voltage variations are +2.4v+4.2V. A diagrammatic illustration of the tabular data is given in Fig. 1 .

The essential factors are frequency, impedance, and the circuit type used. Based on the SOH testing type, the associated factors are observed. In the above representation, the charging-discharging cycles, temperature monitoring, and SOH are observed using FBG sensors. Conventional battery management uses voltage, current, and temperature sensors. The raw information from the sensors is used in this tabular data, and the same information is used for analysis. In the dataset, not all the fields are accommodated; some values are not observed/missed for random cell count/cycles. The considered factors are responsible for optimal and (low/peak) point classification. Therefore, this tabular data is utilized throughout the article for CMI analysis (Fig. 1).

Battery health monitoring uses two crucial parameters: State of Change (SOC) and State of Health (SOH). Based on these parameters, Li batteries are designed with a battery capacity that plays a vital role in the charging and discharging parts. In this instance, the SOH is the aging of the specific battery where it is charged and discharged must be appropriately observed. The FBG sensor-based technique is introduced because it allows for accurate, real-time tracking of essential parameters like temperature and strain without affecting how the battery works. FBG sensors are far more trustworthy for continuous battery health assessment than standard sensors since they are lightweight, immune to electromagnetic interference, and can work in severe settings. The main benefits are that it is more sensitive, lasts longer, and can sense things over the battery surface. This makes it easier to find early symptoms of battery degradation, which helps to anticipate better the battery's state of health (SOH) and state of charge (SOC). This, in turn, helps to make better maintenance decisions and increases the battery's life compared to traditional monitoring methods. The degradation of batteries is the failure model, and they are discarded. The state of change indicates 0 or 1; if it is fully charged, it is 1. When the charge is running out, it means the SOC is 0. Based on these parameters, the Li batteries are developed, and the extended maintenance index is shown. The proposed scheme is diagrammatically portrayed in Fig. 2 a.

The temperature, voltage, and current sensors are attached to the battery's input and output. The input path sensors measure the charging capacity, heat expelled, and the time taken. The output sensors observe the disseminating (discharging) current and voltage from the outlet paths to ensure the temperature does not exceed the highest range. This provides the smooth operation of the battery based on physical parameters (Fig. 1). This proposal aims to identify the SOH and maintain the index value up to the mark. In this approach, precision is improved where the optimal temperature and voltage are differentiated. The charging and discharging values indicate the factors considered when the operations are detected. The temperature and voltage are established for the battery life and provide the optimal value range. In this process, the requirements of the batteries are observed and tracked, where the voltage, temperature, current, and capacity are detected. The requirement for a Li battery is processed by tracking key parameters such as voltage, current, temperature, and capacity. This compact information is measured using the batteries and the near-placed FBG sensors. The sensors operate using battery power, which collects the same data. FBG sensors mounted on or near the Li-ion battery provide sensor data for the CPMI procedure. These sensors monitor internal voltage and stress by measuring temperature and strain. Raw data are preprocessed for uniformity, including missing value management and normalization. Next, peak temperature, voltage variation, and charge-discharge cycle count are retrieved. A Support Vector Machine (SVM) classifier receives data labeled by health state thresholds (normal, warning, critical). The SVM learns battery behavior patterns from previous labeled data. The trained model predicts health from live sensor inputs. The classifier result generates a CPMI score that quantifies upkeep. Decision rules use this score to assess battery health. Real-time maintenance notifications or battery use recommendations are output (Fig. 2 b).

From this method, the requirement is used to define the tracking of the operation and provide the battery life to a particular extent. In this processing step, the battery health monitoring is observed in AI and IoT. Thus, the maintenance index states the battery's life and provides the average voltage for the additional run-time. The discussion is briefed as a comparative analysis using maintenance level identification, classification ratio, classification time, SOH precision, and assessment rate metrics. As valued previously, the cycles and the dissemination voltage are the variants used. From the related works, SOH-HELM [30], DTV-LSTM [25], and BA-RVM [23] are used in the comparative discussion.

FBG sensors were used in the tests to track the operational status and health of Lithium-ion (Li-ion) batteries. By monitoring critical physical factors like strain and temperature, these sensors provide a non-invasive way to indirectly deduce how the voltage behaves over a battery's charge and discharge cycles.
1)
Measurement Principle and Voltage Inference

FBG sensors don't measure voltage directly; instead, they look for changes in temperature and strain on the battery surface when charged or discharged, and trained models link these readings to voltage. By comparing these changes in strain and temperature with electrical measurements taken simultaneously, a model is created that can predict how the internal voltage would behave based on physical sensor data.
2)
Sensor Placement

To collect relevant and reliable data, Li-ion cells have FBG sensors attached to their exterior at strategic points where mechanical and thermal fluctuations are most likely to occur, such as on the periphery of the electrodes, where most electrochemical reactions occur. At various locations along the surface or housing of the battery, strain and temperature gradients can be monitored. Thanks to their location, the sensors can detect localized stress and heat behavior, which represents the battery's current operational status. The sensors are put on the battery's surface, especially in places that are likely to get hot or stressed, so the battery's health may be monitored in real time without opening it up.

The maintenance level identification improves the state of health in which both operations are considered and provides better temperature and voltage. In this case, the precision is enhanced, which improves the lifespan of the batteries for the computation. Here, classifier learning is introduced and provides a better usage process considering factors for the charging and discharging. Here, the threshold value indicates the SOH prediction, whereas battery usage suggests the battery's requirements. The assessment rate is calculated and estimated better for every step of computation. In this processing step, tracking the key parameter involves the optimization of the precision level, and it is represented as
{
[
(
i a
+
m g
)
∗ O
]
+
T ′
}
. The distance is the central part of this SVM, where the margin is mapped with the operation and considered factors. Here, the threshold level from the FBG sensor reading indicates better computation based on the battery storage condition. The computation time is measured with the SOH and the set of operations and optimality. Based on these, the assessment rate is calculated where it shows better processing in this work. The comparative analysis highlights that CMIS is more efficient in training than SOH-HELM [30], DTV-LSTM [25], but less efficient than BA-RVM [23]. All models have similar classification times of
O
( d )
per data point. CMIS and DTV-LSTM have higher execution times, with CMIS offering a more balanced training approach despite similar memory usage across all models. CMIS achieves a throughput of about 10 classifications per second, offering predictable real-time performance compared to models with varying throughput based on complexity, as shown in Table II .

Table III provides FBG sensors and SVM kernel type for classification on the health of lithium-ion batteries. FBG sensors are put on battery parts likely to experience temperature and strain variations to get reliable data. They can measure temperatures from −40 °C to +85 °C and voltages up to 5V, which the batteries need. The SVM uses linear or RBF kernels, and during training, parameters like gamma and cost are set to their best values. We set classification levels based on sensor data from charging cycles that we know are accurate. The system gets the correct answer around 75 % of the time. The sensor is accurate for temperature to within ±1 °C; for voltage, it's proper to within a few millivolts. With these options, you can easily keep track of the battery's state of health and charge in real time. This method helps with timely maintenance, which makes the battery last longer and work better.

FBG sensors have problems such as calibration drift, environmental interference, and strain and temperature cross-sensitivity. Regular recalibration and baseline referencing helped reduce calibration drift. Protective packaging, temperature correction, and signal filtering are used to deal with environmental concerns like humidity and vibration. Dual-FBG configurations and calibration techniques isolate the impacts and deal with cross-sensitivity problems. Also, precise fiber routing and protective coatings helped lower the chance of mechanical damage. These procedures ensured that the sensor readings were always correct and consistent during the experiments.

The disadvantages related to sensors that can impair measurement accuracy are sensor drift, calibration mistakes, and sensitivity to environmental changes. The disadvantages include noise and signal interference that make the data less reliable. FBG sensors are quite accurate; however, they can be impacted by strain and temperature cross-sensitivity, which can make measurements wrong. To reduce problems, calibrate and install sensors correctly. FBG sensors are usually accurate, with temperature readings within ±1 °C and voltage readings within a few millivolts. However, the accuracy relies on the quality of the device and the environment.

The maximum performance (95.2 % accuracy, 94.6 % F1-score) is achieved with temperature and strain inputs, as shown in Table IV Ablation 1. These properties are complementary. SOH classification accuracy drops 7.6 % when temperature data is removed, but 12.1 % when strain is excluded, indicating strain signals are more important, although both are necessary for optimal results.

SVM kernel and hyperparameter effects are compared in Table V Ablation 2. The RBF kernel with 0.5 and 0.1 demonstrates optimal performance (94.8 % accuracy, 93.6 % F1-score) with reasonable latency. Raising
γ
to 0.5 results in overfitting and decreased generalization, while the linear kernel falters because of inadequate non-linear representation. These findings stress the need for kernel selection and optimization for good diagnostic accuracy.

This article introduced the classifier-pursued maintenance indicator scheme to improve the SOH estimation of Li batteries using Fiber Bragg Grating (FBG) sensors. This SOH is estimated using charging and discharging cycles with the temperature and voltage conditions. The conventional support vector classifier is used in this scheme to improve the optimal condition-based detection of different considered factors. For maximum considered factor combinations, the optimal and non-optimal range of the charging state is computed based on voltage and temperature. The classifier identifies these optimal and non-optimal cases and is grouped for further training. The maintenance index is defined periodically using the maximum grouped classifications for a complete battery operation cycle. Therefore, SOH and SOC are contradictorily used in classifying interval operations and sustainable random trial assessments. From the comparative analysis, these observations are presented: 13.79 % high maintenance index identification, 13.76 % high precision (under different cycles), 10.48 % less classification time, and 10.07 % high classification ratio (under different voltage).

Developing a framework for real-time acquisition and processing using IoT technologies could significantly enhance the applicability of the methodology. This would allow for continuous monitoring of battery health and immediate adjustments to operational parameters based on real-time insights.
